title: Sharks, Alligators, Piranhas, and the Top 10 Garbage Fish Movies
format: Brian Raftery stops by to preview his Vietnam War movie podcast, then Chris Ryan joins Sean to trace 'garbage fish' cinema from Jaws through Meg 2.

segments.heading: Brian Raftery previews 'Do We Get to Win This Time?'
segments.note: his new podcast on Hollywood and the Vietnam War

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.note: Raftery rewatched it for the podcast and still isn't sure what to make of the last third; Vietnamese American viewers he interviewed love the film but note its Vietnamese characters are barely named.

films.title: Full Metal Jacket
films.note: One of the titles Raftery says defined watching Vietnam movies as a kid, alongside Platoon.

films.title: Platoon
films.note: Cited as required viewing for Raftery's generation.

films.title: Hamburger Hill
films.note: Raftery watched it at 13, unsupervised, and says it traumatized him.

films.title: The Deer Hunter
films.note: One of the films Amanda names as the obvious first-thought Vietnam movies.

films.title: Coming Home
films.note: Also named by Amanda as a foundational entry.

films.title: Born on the Fourth of July
films.note: Rounds out Amanda's list of the obvious titles.

films.title: All Quiet on the Western Front
films.note: Cited as one of the rare recent examples of the kind of big, ambitious war epic studios used to make constantly.

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: Named alongside All Quiet on the Western Front as a modern exception to the dead war-epic genre.

films.title: Brotherhood of Death
films.note: A Tarantino recommendation Raftery finally tracked down: three Black Vietnam vets return home to find their town infiltrated by the KKK. Raftery calls the last 10 minutes electrifying.

films.title: Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse
films.note: Eleanor Coppola's documentary of the Apocalypse Now shoot, which Raftery interviewed her for; he says he'd happily watch a 12-hour cut of the raw footage.

films.title: The Green Berets
films.note: John Wayne's pro-war, by-the-book Vietnam movie, which Raftery says hovers over the whole podcast since it shaped how Oliver Stone and Ron Kovic saw the war growing up.

segments.heading: The Garbage Fish Canon
segments.note: Chris Ryan joins Sean to define and trace the subgenre, prompted by Meg 2

films.title: Jaws
films.note: The genre's founding text and, they note, an unusually rare case of a genuine classic spawning decades of imitators.

films.title: Creature from the Black Lagoon
films.note: Named as a pre-Jaws predecessor of the subgenre.

films.title: 20,000 Leagues Under the Sea
films.note: Also cited as an early predecessor.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Guillermo del Toro's Oscar winner, which Chris calls a garbage fish movie underneath the prestige — a direct riff on Creature From the Black Lagoon.

films.title: Frogs
films.note: Ray Milland plays a nature-hating estate owner besieged by killer frogs, with Sam Elliott, pre-mustache, as a photographer. Predates Jaws and used real frogs.

films.title: Orca
films.note: Michael Anderson's killer-whale movie with Richard Harris and Charlotte Rampling, shot with real orcas. Chris calls it the first artistically strange, upsetting post-Jaws entry.

films.title: Piranha
films.note: Joe Dante's satirical entry, written by John Sayles, ahead of Dante's run into Gremlins.

films.title: Piranha II: The Spawning
films.note: James Cameron's directorial debut, which he later tried to disown. The piranha fly out of the water in this one.

films.title: Alligator
films.note: Paired with Piranha as the era's low-budget gator entry.

films.title: Jaws 2
films.note: The sequel they'll defend as genuinely good, not great, and a legitimate rewatch.

films.title: Jaws 3-D
films.note: Set in a SeaWorld-style park and directed by Joe Alves, Jaws's production designer, in his only directing credit.

films.title: Jaws: The Revenge
films.note: Ellen Brody is followed to the Bahamas by a shark that seems to remember her, with Michael Caine and Mario Van Peebles. Poster tagline: 'This time it's personal.'

films.title: Leviathan
films.note: George P. Cosmatos's 'Alien underwater,' with Peter Weller, Richard Crenna, and Stan Winston creature effects. Chris names it his all-time favorite of the genre.

films.title: Deep Blue Sea
films.note: Genetically enhanced sharks turn on a research crew hunting an Alzheimer's cure, and deliver one of the most quoted shark kills in movie history.

films.title: Lake Placid
films.note: Steve Miner's giant-gator comedy with Bill Pullman, Bridget Fonda, and Oliver Platt.

films.title: Anaconda
films.note: Added to the canon mid-conversation after Chris's wife rules that a snake attacking from water counts.

films.title: Ghost Ship
films.note: Adjacent to the genre, but Chris says it plays more as a supernatural movie than a garbage fish one.

films.title: Open Water
films.note: A three-actor, real-shark independent production loosely based on a true story of two divers stranded at sea.

films.title: Open Water 2: Adrift
films.note: Connected to the original mostly by title — divers can't get back onto their boat after leaving the ladder down.

films.title: Rogue
films.note: Greg McLean's Australian crocodile movie with Radha Mitchell and Michael Vartan, part of what they call an Australian revolution in the genre.

films.title: The Reef
films.note: A similar setup to Open Water: a capsized boat and sharks between the survivors and the shore.

films.title: The Shallows
films.note: Jaume Collet-Serra's film with Blake Lively stranded on a rock, part of a run of mid-budget genre hits Chris wishes studios still funded.

films.title: Crawl
films.note: Alexandre Aja's hurricane-and-gators movie with Kaya Scodelario, reportedly a favorite of Quentin Tarantino's for its year.

films.title: Underwater
films.note: William Eubank's Mariana Trench monster movie with Kristen Stewart, buried by a pandemic release.

films.title: The Meg
films.note: Liked well enough, though the CGI shark drew complaints.

films.title: Meg 2: The Trench
films.note: The new release that prompted this whole conversation.

films.title: Sharknado
films.note: Explicitly carved out of the canon as a self-aware, purposefully cheap Syfy phenomenon rather than a real garbage fish movie.

films.title: The Host
films.note: Discussed as a borderline case; Chris calls it a foot in both ponds with Godzilla, and ultimately not quite garbage fish.

films: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
Close Encounters of the Third Kind
